coredata.dc:title: Preparation of Lysosome-targeting Carbon Dots and Its Application in Cell Imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2021 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: FAN, Zi-Yan
LIU, Zheng-Jie
ZHANG, Rui-Long
HAN, Guang-Mei
ZHANG, Zhong-Ping
coredata.dc:description: The lysosome-targeting carbon dots (CDs) with excellent water solubility and strong blue emission were synthesized through a one-step functional modification for long-term lysosome imaging in living cells. Based on the carboxyl-rich CDs synthesized by oxidizing carbon black in refluxing HNO3, then N,N-dimethylethylenediamine (DMEDA) was chemically linked onto the CDs through acylation reaction to obtain DMEDA modified carbon dots (M-CDs). The infrared spectrum of M-CDs showed the characteristic peak of acylamide (O=C–NH–) at 1658 cm−1, indicating the successful modification of DMEDA. The quantum yield of M-CDs was 15.6%, which was 27 times higher than the initial carboxyl rich CDs, meeting the requirement of cell imaging. The probe entered cells through the temperature-dependent endocytosis way and the co-localization experiment with commercial dye Lyso-Tracker red showed that the probe localized in lysosomes and could be used as a universal lysosome tracker in different cell lines. Moreover, compared to commercially available Lyso-Tracker blue, M-CDs were more photostable under UV light. The fluorescence of M-CDs was still brightness after time-lapsed imaging by confocal microscopy for 120 min with an interval of 5 min in living cells, implying that the M-CDs could be used for long-term imaging of lysosomes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Functional modification
N,N-dimethylethylenediamine
Lysosome target
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: CHINESE JOURNAL OF ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY Volume 49, Issue 7, July 2021 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: Chinese J. Anal. Chem., 2021, 49(7): 1208â€“1217 ________________________ Received 24 November 2020; accepted 26 April 2021 *Corresponding author. Email: gmhan@ahu.edu.cn This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22074001, 21874137, 21974001, 21775001) and the Science and Technology Service Network Initiative of Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. KFJ-STS-ZDTP-080). Copyright Â© 2021, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. Originally published in 2021 in Chinese by Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. All rights reserved. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-2040(21)60108-1 RESEARCH PAPER Preparation of Lysosome-targeting Carbon Dots and Its Application in Cell Imaging FAN Zi-Yan1, LIU Zheng-Jie1, ZHANG Rui-Long1, HAN Guang-Mei1,*, ZHANG Zhong-Ping1,2 1 Information Materials and Intelligent Sensing Laboratory of Anhui Province, Institutes of Physical Science and Information Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China 2 Key Laboratory of Photovoltaic and Energy Conservation Materials, Institute of Solid State Physics, HFIPS, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei 230031, China Abstract: The lysosome-targeting carbon dots (CDs) with excellent water solubility and strong blue emission were synthesized through a one-step functional modification for long-term lysosome imaging in living cells. Based on the carboxyl-rich CDs synthesized by oxidizing carbon black in refluxing HNO3, then N,N-dimethylethylenediamine (DMEDA) was chemically linked onto the CDs through acylation reaction to obtain DMEDA modified carbon dots (M-CDs). The infrared spectrum of M-CDs showed the characteristic peak of acylamide (O=Câ€“NHâ€“) at 1658 cmâ€“1, indicating the successful modification of DMEDA. The quantum yield of M-CDs was 15.6%, which was 27 times higher than the initial carboxyl rich CDs, meeting the requirement of cell imaging. The probe entered cells through the temperature-dependent endocytosis way and the co-localization experiment with commercial dye Lyso-Tracker red showed that the probe localized in lysosomes and could be used as a universal lysosome tracker in different cell lines. Moreover, compared to commercially available Lyso-Tracker blue, M-CDs were more photostable under UV light. The fluorescence of M-CDs was still brightness after time-lapsed imaging by confocal microscopy for 120 min with an interval of 5 min in living cells, implying that the M-CDs could be used for long-term imaging of lysosomes. Key Words: Carbon dots; Functional modification; N,N-dimethylethylenediamine; Lysosome target; Cell imaging 1 Introduction Lysosomes are key organelle containing various hydrolases for the degradation of intracellular substances and play vital roles in maintaining cellular homeostasis through autophagy, apoptosis and other biological processes, such as the degradation of biomacromolecules, elimination of senescent organelles and repair of plasma membranes, etc. [1â€“3] . Lysosomal dysfunction can disrupt intracellular homeostasis, resulting in lysosomal storage diseases, neurodegeneration, cancer and other diseases [2,3] . Thereby, it is of great significance to real-time monitor the dynamical behaviors of lysosomes in living cells for deeply understanding of their physiological and pathological functions. Compared with traditional electron microscope imaging and electrochemical methods, fluorescent imaging has become a powerful tool for direct lysosome imaging in living systems due to its high sensitivity, simple operation and dynamic tracing ability [4,5] . Recently, many efforts have been devoted on development of lysosome-specific probes [6,7] . Among them, the classical organic molecule probes, such as morpholine and lyso-tracker, are widely used in lysosome imaging [8] , but the inherent photobleaching properties restrict their applications in long- term imaging. As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) are not only more photostable but also possess the distinctive advantages such as good hydrophilicity, tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties and low cytotoxicity [9â€“16] . Thus, CDs have been widely pursued in FAN Zi-Yan et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2021, 49(7): 1208â€“1217 bioimaging and sensing, including biomarkers, cell imaging and drug delivery [17â€“22] . Especially, the heteroatom doping and surface passivation have been appealing methods to tune the PL properties of CDs and improve its biocompatibility and targeting ability. For example, Wu and coworkers [23] synthesized a morpholine modified CDs for lysosome imaging. Moreover, our group [24] realized the specific labeling of nucleus by regulating the surface charge of CDs. Here, based on the carboxy-rich CDs obtained by a chemical oxidation method [25] in refluxing HNO3, the lysosome targeting CDs (M-CDS) were synthesized by chemically linking DMEDA onto the initial carboxy-rich CDs through a simple one-step acylation reaction. The surface passivation by DMEDA not only made a 27-fold enhancement of quantum yield from the carboxy-rich CDs to M-CDS, but also enabled its lysosome-targeting ability. Moreover, with the advantages such as low cytotoxicity and strong photostability, M-CDS were successfully used for long-term imaging of lysosome in living cells, providing a powerful tool for the study of lysosome-related biological processes. 2 Experimental 2.1 Instruments and reagents Fluorescent spectra and absorption spectra were recorded on Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) and UV-2550 spectrometer (Shimadu, Japan), respectively. Infrared spectra were measured by Nicolet FT-IR Nexus 870 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Transmission electron micrographs (TEM) were obtained from a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fluorescence images were acquired with a Leica TCS SP8X laser confocal microscope (Leica, Germany). Zeta potential data were obtained from a Malvern nanoparticle size potentiometer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern, UK). Aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized water from Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, USA). Nitric acid (HNO3), sulfoxide chloride (SOCl2), N,N- dimethylformamide (DMF), N,N-dimethylethylenediamine (DMEDA) were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5- diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were supplied from Beyotime Biotech. Co. Ltd. (China). Fetal bovine serum, penicillin, streptomycin, trypsin, DMEM medium were purchased from Biological Industries (Israel). 2.2 Synthesis of M-CDs The M-CDs were synthesized according to the reported method [25] . First, 0.20 g of carbon black (VXC-72) was oxidized in refluxing HNO3 (50 mL, 6 mol/L) under constant stirring for 24 h, and then cooled down to room temperature. Subsequently, the supernatant was collected and evaporated at 180 Â°C. The carboxyl-rich CDs (20 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL of SOCl2 solution containing 100 ï�­L of anhydrous DMF and refluxed at 80 Â°C for 10 h. After the excess SOCl2 was removed by distillation, 5 mL of DMEDA was added and reacted at 60 Â°C for 24 h. The final product was dispersed in ethanol and rotary steamed to remove the solvent. After repeated three times, the M-CDs was dialyzed in ultrapure water for 10 h, freeze-dried, and finally dissolved in ultrapure water to prepare 1 mg/mL solution. The obtained solution was stored at 4 Â°C for further use. 2.3 Fluorescent spectra and quantum yield measurements of M-CDs The fluorescence spectra of M-CDs (20 Î¼g/mL) at different exciting wavelengths in the range of 340â€“540 nm with an interval of 20 nm were recorded on Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. The quantum yield of M-CDs was measured by using quinine sulfate as a standard [26] . It was known that the fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate was 54.6% in H2SO4 (0.1 mol/L). The UV absorption spectra of the M-CDs and quinine sulphate were measured and try to keep their absorption intensities at 350 nm consistent with each other, and then the fluorescence spectra were also measured. Finally, the quantum yield of M-CDs was calculated according to the following equation: QYsample = QYaÂ·(Asample/Aa)Â·(Fa/Fsample)Â·(Î·sample 2 /Î·a 2 ) (1) F = 1 â€“ 10 â€“D (2) where, QYa is the quantum yield of quinine sulphate (54.6%), A is the integrated area of the fluorescence spectrum, F is the absorption function, D is the UV absorption value and Î· is the refractive index of the solvent. 2.4 Photostability of M-CDs The photostability of M-CDs was measured using fluorescence spectrophotometer. The quartz cuvettes containing M-CDs solution and lysosomal commercial dye Lyso-Tracker blue were placed on a GL-3120 benchtop UV analyzer (power 48 W, wavelength 365 nm) for continuous irradiation, and the fluorescence spectra were measured at regular intervals, and the changes of fluorescence intensity over time were recorded. 2.5 Cytotoxicity of M-CDs The cytotoxicity of M-CDs was determined by MTT (3-(4,5- dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide) assay. HeLa (human cervical cancer) cells were seeded in 96-well plates with 100 Î¼L of cell resuspension per well and incubated with 5% CO2 at 37 Â°C for 24 h. The old culture medium was removed and the cells were incubated with fresh FAN Zi-Yan et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2021, 49(7): 1208â€“1217 DMEM medium containing different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 and 80 Î¼g/mL) of M-CDs probes for 4 h, respectively. Alternatively, the cells were incubated with DMEM medium containing 30 Î¼g/mL M-CDs for various periods (0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 18, 24 and 36 h). Four parallel wells were set up in each group. For MTT assay, the cells were further incubated with 100 Î¼L of DMEM containing 0.5 mg/mL MTT for 4 h. Finally, 100 Î¼L of DMSO solution was added into each well, and the 96-well plates were shaken for 5 min at medium speed to dissolve the crystals and obtain a uniform color. Then the absorption value at 492 nm was measured using a microplate reader (680, Bio-Rad, USA). The experiments were repeated three times. 2.6 Fluorescent imaging of M-CDs in living cells HeLa cells were seeded in glass-bottomed confocal dishes and incubated with DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% streptomycin and 1% penicillin at 37 Â°C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h. Subsequently, the fresh DMEM medium containing M-CDs (30 Î¼g/mL) was added into the cells and incubated for 1 h. The cells were wash-free and directly imaged under confocal microscope with excitation wavelength of 405 nm. For co-localization experiment, the HeLa cells were washed with PBS three times after incubated with M-CDs probe (30 Î¼g/mL) for 1 h and further incubated with commercial dye Lyso-Tracker red (10 Î¼mol/L) for 20 min under protecting from light. The cells were washed with PBS three times and imaged by confocal microscopy with excitation wavelengths of 405 nm and 543 nm, respectively. 2.7 Ability of M-CDs to localize lysosomes in different cell lines A549 (human lung cancer cells), MCF-7 (human breast cancer cells), SY5Y (human neuroblastoma cells) and QSG (human hepatocytes) cells were incubated with DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% streptomycin and 1% penicillin at 37 Â°C in a 5% CO2 incubator overnight. The fresh DMEM medium containing M-CDs (30 Î¼g/mL) was added into the cells and incubated for 1 h. After being washed three times with PBS, the cells were subsequently incubated with Lyso-Tracker red (10 Î¼mol/L) for 20 min in the dark. After carefully washing, fluorescent imaging was performed on a confocal microscope at excitation wavelengths of 405 nm and 543 nm, respectively. 2.8 Long-term imaging of intracellular lysosomes by M-CDs HeLa cells were incubated as described in Section 2.5. The cells were washed three times with PBS after incubated with DMEM medium containing M-CDs (30 Î¼g/mL), and then fresh DMEM medium was added. Subsequently, the cells were placed on a live cell workstation for time-scan imaging with a scan interval of 5 min for 120 min at 405 nm excitation. 3 Results and discussion 3.1 Synthesis and characterization of probe The synthetic route of CDs is shown in Fig.1. First, the carbon black as precursor was exfoliated and oxidized by concentrated HNO3 to prepare the carbonyl-rich CDs. However, the fluorescent quantum yield was low. The surface passivation is an effective modification method to improve the quantum yield of CDs. Here, DMEDA was modified onto the edge of CDs through acylation reaction under the catalysis of SOCl2, and finally the strong blue emissive CDs named as M-CDs were obtained, satisfying the image requirements. To certify the successful modification of DMEDA, the characterizations of the prepared CDs were performed. As shown in Fig.2A and Fig.2B, the average diameters of CDs still kept about 3 nm after modification and exhibited good dispersibility, but the zeta-potential of CDs before and after modification obviously changed (Fig.2C). In buffer solution (pH 5.0), the CDs before modification showed strong negative potential (â€“11.40 mV), which was in agreement with the existence of carbonyl groups, whereas the M-CDsâ€™ potential was as high as 3.38 mV, indicating that the DMEDA was modified onto the CDs. Fig.1 Synthesis route of N,N-dimethylethylenediamine modified carbon dots (M-CDs) FAN Zi-Yan et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2021, 49(7): 1208â€“1217 Fig.2 TEM images of (A) initial carbon dots obtained by HNO3 oxidation and (B) M-CDs modified by DMEDA; (C) Zeta potential changes of carbon dots before and after modification Further, IR spectra were used to characterize the M-CDs (Fig.3). The original CDs had the intrinsic peak of â€“COOH at 1710 cm â€“1 , vibration peak of Câ€“O(H) at 1252 cm â€“1 and vibration peak of â€“OH at 3500â€“3000 cm â€“1 . After modification by DMEDA, the intrinsic vibration peak of â€“COOH disappeared, while the vibration peak of the amide bond at 1658 cm â€“1 , the vibration peak of Câ€“N bond at 1130 cm â€“1 and the vibration peak of Nâ€“H at 3500 cm â€“1 appeared. The above results proved that DNEDA was chemically linked onto the surface of CDs through acylation reaction. 3.2 Fluorescent properties of M-CDs Surface passivation is a key factor to improve PL efficiency of CDs. Yang et al. [27] first realized in vivo imaging of CDs using PEG-passivated CDs. Here, by using quinine sulfate as a standard, the quantum yield of M-CDs was 15.6%, which was about 27 times higher than that of the initial CDs before modification and passivation. The results suggested that the PL efficiency of CDs was effectively improved by DMEDA modification. Furthermore, we analyzed the ultraviolet and fluorescence spectra of M-CDs. As shown in Fig.4A, the M-CDs had a strong absorption in the ultraviolet region. Under the excitation of 380 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission of M-CDs was at 468 nm. Under the irradiation of a 365-nm UV lamp, M-CDs solution emitted bright blue fluorescence (Inset in Fig.4A). M-CDs also showed the properties of fluorescence emission dependent on excitation wavelength (Fig.4B). With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission peaks of M-CDs shifted to 600 nm, whereas its fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly. 3.3 Cytotoxicity of M-CDs MTT assay was used to measure the cytotoxicity of M-CDs, and the results showed that the cell viability was maintained Fig.3 IR spectra of (a) initial carbon dots obtained by HNO3 oxidation and (b) M-CDs modified by DMEDA Fig.4 (A) UV-vis absorption spectrum (a), excitation spectrum (b) and fluorescence spectrum (c) of M-CDs. Inset: the photograph of M-CDs under ultraviolent light; (B) Fluorescence spectra of M-CDs at different excitation wavelengths of 340, 360, 380, 400, 420, 440, 460, 480, 500, 520 and 540 nm FAN Zi-Yan et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2021, 49(7): 1208â€“1217 above 85% after incubation with M-CDs (0â€“80 Î¼g/mL) for 4 h. In parallel, when the HeLa cells were co-incubated with the M-CDs (30 Î¼g/mL) for different time periods (0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 18, 24 and 36 h), the survival rate of cells was close to 100% within 12 h. Even the incubation time was extended to 36 h, the survival rate of the cells remained at about 90%. These results indicated that M-CDs had very low cytotoxicity and could be used for live cell imaging. 3.4 Fluorescent imaging of M-CDs in living cells Based on the low cytotoxicity of M-CDs, we further studied the distribution of M-CDs in living cells. As shown in Fig.5, bright blue fluorescence was detected in HeLa cells after incubation with M-CDs for 1 h under 405 nm excitation (Fig.5A2), while almost no fluorescence was detected after incubation with non-modification CDs (Fig.5B2), agreeing with our previously reported results [24] . Similarly, no fluorescence was detected in the control cells without M-CDs treatment (Fig.5C2), indicating that spontaneous background fluorescence was not generated under this imaging condition. The above results confirmed that M-CDs could enter cells quickly and met the needs of cell imaging. The co-localization of M-CDs with commercial dye was also performed. As shown in Fig.6, the bright blue emission of M-CDs was almost completely overlapped with red fluorescence of Lyso-Tracker in live cells. Based on the scatterplot of fluorescence signal positions (Fig.6D), the Manders' overlap coefficients (MOC) of the two channels were calculated as 0.93 by the Image J software, indicating that the M-CDs were localized in lysosomes, which was different from the report that the blue CDs modified by ethylenediamine mainly distributed in the cytoplasm and could enter the nucleoli [28] . This may be due to that DMEDA was more alkaline than ethylenediamine, making M-CDs easier to enter into the acidic (about pH 5) lysosome, thus realizing the specific targeting of the lysosome in living cells. The cellular uptake mechanism of M-CDs was further studied through endocytosis inhibitor experiments. First, M-CDs probe-loaded HeLa cells were incubated at 37 Â°C and 4 Â°C, respectively, as shown in Fig.7A and Fig.7B. The strong blue fluorescence in cells cultured at 37 Â°C was detected, while nearly no fluorescence was observed in cells cultured at 4 Â°C, suggesting that the M-CDs entered into cells by a temperature-dependent active pathway. Furthermore, when the cells were treated with endocytosis inhibitors including two lysosomotropic agents (ammonium chloride and chloroquine), and chathrin-mediated inhibitor (chlorpromazine), as shown in Fig.7Câ€“Fig.7E, the fluorescence intensity in the cells was also significantly reduced, indicating that M-CDs mainly entered the cells through endocytosis and localized in lysosome. 3.5 Universality of M-CDs as a lysosome tracker We further investigated the lysosome localization ability of M-CDs in different cell lines. In addition to HeLa cells, four other cell lines were selected including tumor cells (A549, Fig.5 Fluorescent images of HeLa cells incubated with M-CDs (A1â€“A3) and unmodified initial carbon dot (B1â€“B3) for 1 h, respectively. The cells incubated with normal medium are set as the blank. Fig.6 Colocalization experiment of M-CDs with a commercial dye Lyso-Tracker red: (A) Fluorescent image of M-CDs; (B) Fluorescent image of Lyso-Tracker red; (C) Merge image of (A) and (B); (D) Co-localization scatterplot and overlap coefficient. M-CDs channel, excitation at 405 nm; Lyso-Tracker red channel, excitation at 543 nm FAN Zi-Yan et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2021, 49(7): 1208â€“1217 Fig.7 Fluorescent images of HeLa cells incubated with M-CDs at different culture conditions: (A) 37 Â°C; (B) 4 Â°C; (C) NH4Cl; (D) Chloroquine; (E) Chlorpromazine. (F) Relative fluorescence intensity of M-CDs in HeLa cells after different treatments MCF-7 QY5Y) and normal cells (QSG). As shown in Fig.8, after incubation with M-CDs for 1 h, bright blue fluorescence was detected in all these cell lines under 405 nm excitation (Fig.8A2â€“Fig.8D2). Moreover, the fluorescences of M-CDs Fig.8 Co-localization experiments of M-CDs with Lyso-Tracker red in different cell lines including A549 cell (A1â€“A4), MCF-7 cell (B1â€“B4), SY5Y cell (C1â€“C4) and QSG cell (D1â€“D4) FAN Zi-Yan et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2021, 49(7): 1208â€“1217 were well overlapped with that of Lyso-Tracker red (Fig.8A4â€“Fig.8D4), confirming that M-CDs had a specificity to lysosomes and could be used as a universal probe for lysosome localization. 3.6 Long-term imaging of lysosomes in living cells Thanks to the high PL efficiency and low cytotoxicity of M-CDS, the long-term imaging ability of M-CDS to lysosomes was investigated. As one of the most important factors for long-term imaging, the photostability of M-CDs was first tested. As shown in Fig.9, the fluorescence intensity of M-CDS remained above 80% under continuous UV irradiation within 4 h. However, under the same experiment conditions, the fluorescence intensity of commercial Lyso-Tracker blue sharply decreased and completely quenched within 10 min, indicating that the M-CDs as a nano-fluorescent probe were more photostable than the traditional molecule dye and suitable for long-term cell imaging. The probe-loaded HeLa cells were placed on a live cell workstation for time-scan imaging with a scan interval of 5 min at 405 nm excitation. As shown in Fig.10Aâ€“Fig.10H, the cell images of representative time points showed that bright blue fluorescence was still detected in the lysosome of cells after 25 scans and the fluorescence intensity still kept 80% of the initial one in 120 min (Fig.10I), which indicated that M-CDS could be used as an ideal lysosome probe for long-term imaging to track the dynamics of intracellular lysosomes in real time. Fig.9 Photostability of (a) M-CDs and (b) Lyso-Tracker blue under UV irradiation (365 nm, 48 W) Fig.10 (Aâ€“H) Time-lapse imaging of M-CDs (30 Î¼g/mL) in HeLa cells and (I) fluorescence intensity variation of M-CDs during the time-lapse imaging 4 Conclusions The M-CDs was prepared through a one-step functional modification with DMEDA. The as-synthesized M-CDs not only featured by high quantum yield, low cytotoxicity and strong photostability, but also could specifically target lysosomes. The fluorescence imaging of different cell lines showed that M-CDs could be used as a universal tracker for lysosome localization. More importantly, the M-CDs could be used for the long-term imaging of lysosomes, providing a powerful tool for the study of morphology and quantitation of lysosomes under physiological and pathological conditions. Conflict of interests The authors declare no competing financial interest. References [1] Zhang X, Wang J, Li X G, Wang D H. B, 2016, 4(4): 779â€“784 M-CDs during the time-lapse imaging 4 Conclusions The M-CDs was prepared through a one-step functional modification with DMEDA. The as-synthesized M-CDs not only featured by high quantum yield, low cytotoxicity and strong photostability, but also could specifically target lysosomes. The fluorescence imaging of different cell lines showed that M-CDs could be used as a universal tracker for lysosome localization. More importantly, the M-CDs could be used for the long-term imaging of lysosomes, providing a powerful tool for the study of morphology and quantitation of lysosomes under physiological and pathological conditions. Conflict of interests The authors declare no competing financial interest. References [1] Zhang X, Wang J, Li X G, Wang D H. Cancer Lett., 2018, 439: 39â€“46 [2] Carmona Gutierrez D, Hughes A L, Madeo F, Ruckenstuhl C. Ageing Res. Rev., 2016, 32: 2â€“12 [3] Davidson S M, Vander Heiden M G. Annu. Rev. Pharmacol., 2017, 57(1): 481â€“507 [4] Dong C B, Li F C, Dong C Q, Cai P, Ren J C. J. Anal. Sci., 2020, (05): 695â€“700 [5] Koch M, Symvoulidis P, Ntziachristos V. Nat. Photonics, 2018, 12(9): 505â€“515 [6] Xu Y F, Wan W. Chin. J. Cell Biol., 2019, 41(07): 1428â€“1436 [7] Bonam S R, Wang F, Muller S. Nat. Rev. Drug Discovery, 2019, 18(12): 923â€“948 [8] Wang Y, Huang C S, Jia N Q. Prog

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22074001, 21874137, 21974001, 21775001) and the Science and Technology Service Network Initiative of Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. KFJ-STS-ZDTP-080).

head.ce:title: Preparation of Lysosome-targeting Carbon Dots and Its Application in Cell Imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Information Materials and Intelligent Sensing Laboratory of Anhui Province, Institutes of Physical Science and Information Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Information Materials and Intelligent Sensing Laboratory of Anhui Province
Institutes of Physical Science and Information Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Information Materials and Intelligent Sensing Laboratory of Anhui Province, Institutes of Physical Science and Information Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photovoltaic and Energy Conservation Materials, Institute of Solid State Physics, HFIPS, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei 230031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photovoltaic and Energy Conservation Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Photovoltaic and Energy Conservation Materials, Institute of Solid State Physics, HFIPS, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei 230031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The lysosome-targeting carbon dots (CDs) with excellent water solubility and strong blue emission were synthesized through a one-step functional modification for long-term lysosome imaging in living cells. Based on the carboxyl-rich CDs synthesized by oxidizing carbon black in refluxing HNO3, then N,N-dimethylethylenediamine (DMEDA) was chemically linked onto the CDs through acylation reaction to obtain DMEDA modified carbon dots (M-CDs). The infrared spectrum of M-CDs showed the characteristic peak of acylamide (O=C–NH–) at 1658 cm−1, indicating the successful modification of DMEDA. The quantum yield of M-CDs was 15.6%, which was 27 times higher than the initial carboxyl rich CDs, meeting the requirement of cell imaging. The probe entered cells through the temperature-dependent endocytosis way and the co-localization experiment with commercial dye Lyso-Tracker red showed that the probe localized in lysosomes and could be used as a universal lysosome tracker in different cell lines. Moreover, compared to commercially available Lyso-Tracker blue, M-CDs were more photostable under UV light. The fluorescence of M-CDs was still brightness after time-lapsed imaging by confocal microscopy for 120 min with an interval of 5 min in living cells, implying that the M-CDs could be used for long-term imaging of lysosomes.

A lysosome-targeting carbon dots (M-CDs) with strong blue emission was obtained by chemically linking N,N-dimethylethylenediamine onto the carboxyl-rich carbon dots through acylation reaction, achieving the long-term imaging of lysosomes in living cells.
